steps.prompt: How high does the ladder reach?

graphic.name: ladder
graphic.caption: A ladder leans against a wall. The wall, the ground, and the ladder make a right triangle — and the three sides are linked by one exact rule.

steps.body:
A right triangle has one square corner (a 90° angle). The two sides that meet there are the legs, [a](blue) and [b](orange).
The side across from the square corner — always the longest — is the [hypotenuse c](green).
This whole lesson is one rule that links the two legs to the hypotenuse.

steps.prompt: Drag a corner and watch the sides
steps.subtitle: The longest side always sits opposite the biggest angle. When one corner is a square corner (90°), that longest side is the hypotenuse. Drag and see the lengths change together.

steps.intro: A skate ramp rises [6 m](blue) straight up over an [8 m](orange) run along the ground. Find the length of the [sloping ramp](green) — the hypotenuse — one move at a time.

parts.note: 6² = 36 — the area of a square built on the rise.
That sum is the square of the hypotenuse, so c² = 100.

parts.prompt: Finally, take the square root to get the ramp length: √100 = ?
parts.note: The ramp is 10 m — longer than either leg, but shorter than 6 + 8 = 14.

steps.doneNote: That's the whole method: square the legs, add, then take the square root.

steps.subtitle: In any right triangle, the squares of the two legs add up to the square of the hypotenuse: [a](blue)² + [b](orange)² = [c](green)². Fill in the 3-4-5 case.

feedback.incorrect: Square each leg and add: 9 + 16. Then take the square root of that total to get c.

steps.doneNote: 3² + 4² = 25, and √25 = 5. The two small squares exactly fill the big one.

stages: Take four copies of our right triangle, each with legs a and b and hypotenuse c.
Pack all four into a square of side a + b. The tilted gap left in the middle is a square on the hypotenuse, so its area is c².
Now slide the same four triangles into the corners a different way. The leftover space is two squares — one a by a, the other b by b.
Same four triangles, same big square, so the two leftover areas must be equal: a² + b² = c².

steps.subtitle: Square the two shorter sides and add; if that equals the longest side squared, it's a right triangle.

cards.statement: For a right triangle, c = a + b.
cards.note: No — that skips the squaring and the square root. c = √(a² + b²), which is always less than a + b.
4 + 9 = 13, but 4² = 16. Since 13 ≠ 16, it's not quite a right triangle.

cards.statement: The hypotenuse is always the longest side.
cards.note: It sits opposite the 90° angle — the biggest angle — so it faces the longest side.

steps.doneNote: Square first, add, then take the square root — and the hypotenuse is always the longest side.

problems.prompt: A right triangle has legs of 3 and 4. Find the hypotenuse.

feedback.incorrect: In a right triangle, leg² + leg² = hypotenuse².

problems.strongHint: The hypotenuse c has c² = 3² + 4² = 25. Take the square root.

problems.prompt: A right triangle has a leg of 5 and a hypotenuse of 13. Find the other leg.

feedback.incorrect: Rearrange a² + b² = c²: the missing leg² = hypotenuse² − known leg².

problems.strongHint: The missing leg b has b² = 13² − 5² = 144. Take the square root.

problems.prompt: A right triangle has legs of 8 and 15. Find the hypotenuse.

feedback.incorrect: In a right triangle, leg² + leg² = hypotenuse².

problems.strongHint: The hypotenuse c has c² = 8² + 15² = 289. Take the square root.
